summary.source: TfL Santander Cycles annual trips + TfL cycling infrastructure assets

hero: 8,886,295 valid trips form the annual evidence base.
800 live docking stations define the network geography before any flow is drawn.
network: Hyde Park Corner, Hyde Park sits inside a dense central access zone.
City of London has the highest trip intensity, while Westminster carries the largest annual volume.
rhythm: Weekend demand peaks around 13:00 and follows a different daily rhythm.
commute: Waterloo Station 3, Waterloo to Queen Street 1, Bank is the strongest visible weekday 08:00 link.
Ackroyd Drive, Bow to Maplin Street, Mile End is one of the strongest recurring commute corridors across the wider peak window.
Morning flows compress the network into a few dominant links rather than a uniform web.
weekend: Hyde Park Corner, Hyde Park to Hyde Park Corner, Hyde Park becomes prominent on weekend midday.
Weekend movement redistributes demand away from a pure workday spine.
infrastructure: 52 stations fall into the high-demand, low-support mismatch class.
Newham currently has the strongest mismatch signal.
conclusion: Temporal rhythm and infrastructure support do not align evenly across the network.
The strongest planning question is not only where bikes move, but where support lags behind demand.

methodNotes.lowStressProxy: Low-stress accessibility is represented as a proxy based on protected lane proximity, protected lane share within 500m, and nearby traffic calming assets. It is not a full route-based accessibility model.
